3. Inference for simple linear regression

MATH 4210, Chapter 3

Leer en espanol

In 2.2 Least squares from first principles we fit a line to the Toluca Company’s 25 production runs and got a slope of b1=3.5702b_1 = 3.5702b1​=3.5702 hours per unit of lot size. The engineers were pleased, and then they asked the questions any careful person asks about a number: how sure are you? A different 25 runs would have given a slightly different slope. Could the true slope really be zero, meaning lot size does not affect hours at all and we are fooling ourselves? And the practical one, the reason they collected the data: if we schedule a new run of 100 units next week, how many hours should we budget, and how far off might that be?

Chapter 2 gave point estimates: single best guesses for the intercept, slope, and error variance. A point estimate with no measure of its uncertainty is half an answer. This chapter supplies the other half. We attach margins of error to the slope and intercept, test whether the slope is really different from zero, and, most importantly for planning, build two very different intervals around a prediction. One says where the average run of a given size lands. The other says where a single new run lands. Figure 1 shows both drawn around the Toluca line, and the gap between them is the single most useful idea in the chapter.

Everything here rests on one added assumption that Chapter 2 introduced but did not yet use for inference: the errors are normally distributed (2.6 Maximum likelihood under normal errors). With that assumption the sampling distributions sketched in 2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem become exact ttt and FFF distributions, and honest intervals and tests follow.

This lesson at a glance

  • What we are doing: Attaching uncertainty to the Toluca fit: confidence intervals and tests for the slope and intercept, the ANOVA table and FFF test, and two different intervals around a prediction.

  • Why we are doing it: A point estimate with no margin of error is half an answer; the engineers need to know whether the true slope could be zero and how many hours to budget for a new run.

  • Main objective: Build and interpret ttt intervals and tests and the ANOVA FFF test, and distinguish a confidence interval for a mean response from a prediction interval for a new observation.

  • What changed from the last chapters: Chapter 2 gave point estimates assuming only that the errors have mean zero, constant variance, and are uncorrelated; this chapter adds the normal-errors assumption (2.6 Maximum likelihood under normal errors), which turns the sampling distributions into exact ttt and FFF. The Toluca fit (b1=3.5702b_1 = 3.5702b1​=3.5702) is unchanged, now carrying inference.

Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

  • Derive the sampling distributions of b0b_0b0​ and b1b_1b1​ under normal errors, and explain why inference uses a ttt distribution with n−2n-2n−2 degrees of freedom.

  • Construct and interpret confidence intervals and hypothesis tests for the slope and intercept, reading each number off software output.

  • Distinguish a confidence interval for a mean response from a prediction interval for a new observation, derive both, and choose the right one for a question.

  • Derive the ANOVA decomposition SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE, build the ANOVA table, and run the FFF test for the slope.

  • Prove that the FFF test equals the square of the ttt test in simple linear regression.

  • Interpret R2R^2R2 correctly, and name what it does and does not measure.

  • Construct a Working-Hotelling confidence band for the whole regression line.

3.1 The sampling distributions of b0b_0b0​ and b1b_1b1​¶

Intuition¶

We already know from 2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem that the slope estimate b1b_1b1​ is unbiased, with variance σ2/Sxx\sigma^2/S_{xx}σ2/Sxx​, where Sxx=∑i(Xi−Xˉ)2S_{xx} = \sum_i (X_i - \bar{X})^2Sxx​=∑i​(Xi​−Xˉ)2 is the spread of the predictor values about their mean Xˉ\bar{X}Xˉ. That tells us where b1b_1b1​ centers and how much it spreads, but not the shape of its distribution. To build an interval we need the shape. The trick is that b1b_1b1​ is a weighted sum of the responses, and a weighted sum of independent normal random variables is itself normal. So the moment we assume normal errors, b1b_1b1​ is exactly normal, and we can standardize it the way intro statistics standardizes a sample mean.

There is one wrinkle. Standardizing needs the true error standard deviation σ\sigmaσ, which we do not know. We replace it with the estimate s=MSEs = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}s=MSE​. Swapping a known constant for a noisy estimate adds a little extra uncertainty, and that extra uncertainty is exactly what turns the normal distribution into a ttt distribution. Figure 2 confirms this: standardize 5000 simulated slopes by their own estimated standard errors, and the pile matches a ttt curve, with slightly heavier tails than the normal.

Formula¶

Under the normal error model Yi=β0+β1Xi+εiY_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_iYi​=β0​+β1​Xi​+εi​ with εi∼iidN(0,σ2)\varepsilon_i \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)εi​∼iidN(0,σ2), the estimators are exactly normal:

b1∼N ⁣(β1, σ2Sxx),b0∼N ⁣(β0, σ2(1n+Xˉ2Sxx)).b_1 \sim N\!\left(\beta_1, \ \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}}\right), \qquad b_0 \sim N\!\left(\beta_0, \ \sigma^2\Big(\tfrac{1}{n} + \tfrac{\bar{X}^2}{S_{xx}}\Big)\right).b1​∼N(β1​, Sxx​σ2​),b0​∼N(β0​, σ2(n1​+Sxx​Xˉ2​)).

In words: each estimator is centered on the parameter it estimates, with the variance we derived in Chapter 2, and now with a normal shape. To standardize either one we need the spread of the estimate. The estimated standard error of the slope (Definition 3.1) supplies it.

Definition 3.1: Estimated standard error

The estimated standard error of an estimator is the estimated standard deviation of its sampling distribution. For the slope and intercept,

s{b1}=MSE/Sxx,s{b0}=MSE(1n+Xˉ2Sxx),s\{b_1\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}/S_{xx}}, \qquad s\{b_0\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}\Big(\tfrac{1}{n} + \tfrac{\bar{X}^2}{S_{xx}}\Big)},s{b1​}=MSE/Sxx​​,s{b0​}=MSE(n1​+Sxx​Xˉ2​)​,

with s=MSEs = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}s=MSE​ standing in for the unknown error standard deviation σ\sigmaσ.

The estimated standard error s{b1}s\{b_1\}s{b1​} is our best guess at how much b1b_1b1​ bounces from one sample to the next. Standardizing b1b_1b1​ with it gives a ttt statistic, the result stated next.

Theorem 3.2: Sampling distributions of b0b_0b0​ and b1b_1b1​

Under the SLR model with normal errors εi∼iidN(0,σ2)\varepsilon_i \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)εi​∼iidN(0,σ2), the estimators are exactly normal, b1∼N(β1,σ2/Sxx)b_1 \sim N(\beta_1, \sigma^2/S_{xx})b1​∼N(β1​,σ2/Sxx​) and b0∼N ⁣(β0,σ2(1/n+Xˉ2/Sxx))b_0 \sim N\!\big(\beta_0, \sigma^2(1/n + \bar{X}^2/S_{xx})\big)b0​∼N(β0​,σ2(1/n+Xˉ2/Sxx​)), and standardizing by the estimated standard error gives

b1−β1s{b1}∼tn−2,b0−β0s{b0}∼tn−2.\frac{b_1 - \beta_1}{s\{b_1\}} \sim t_{n-2}, \qquad \frac{b_0 - \beta_0}{s\{b_0\}} \sim t_{n-2}.s{b1​}b1​−β1​​∼tn−2​,s{b0​}b0​−β0​​∼tn−2​.

In words: subtract the true value and divide by the estimated standard error, and the result follows a ttt distribution with n−2n-2n−2 degrees of freedom. The degrees of freedom are n−2n-2n−2 because that is the divisor in MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE, the two lost degrees paying for b0b_0b0​ and b1b_1b1​ (2.4 Estimating the error variance). This single ttt result powers every interval and test in the rest of the chapter.

That last sentence is worth a picture. Almost everything ahead is one idea, the ttt statistic above, pointed at a different estimate each time. Figure 3 is the map of the chapter on one page: keep it in mind and each new section becomes “the same move, new target.”

Derivation¶

Derivation (the ttt statistic for the slope). We assemble the result from three facts.

Normality. From 2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem, b1=∑ikiYib_1 = \sum_i k_i Y_ib1​=∑i​ki​Yi​ with ki=(Xi−Xˉ)/Sxxk_i = (X_i - \bar{X})/S_{xx}ki​=(Xi​−Xˉ)/Sxx​. Under the normal error model the YiY_iYi​ are independent normal variables, and any linear combination of independent normals is normal. With the mean and variance already derived, b1∼N(β1,σ2/Sxx)b_1 \sim N(\beta_1, \sigma^2/S_{xx})b1​∼N(β1​,σ2/Sxx​). Dividing by the true standard deviation gives a standard normal:

Z=b1−β1σ/Sxx∼N(0,1).Z = \frac{b_1 - \beta_1}{\sigma/\sqrt{S_{xx}}} \sim N(0,1).Z=σ/Sxx​​b1​−β1​​∼N(0,1).

A chi-square, independent of the slope. It can be shown that

SSEσ2=(n−2) MSEσ2∼χn−22,\frac{\mathrm{SSE}}{\sigma^2} = \frac{(n-2)\,\mathrm{MSE}}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{n-2},σ2SSE​=σ2(n−2)MSE​∼χn−22​,

and that this quantity is statistically independent of b1b_1b1​ (indeed of the whole vector (b0,b1)(b_0, b_1)(b0​,b1​)). The clean proof uses the hat matrix and a theorem on quadratic forms in normal vectors; we give it in full in 7.3 The hat matrix, once we have the matrix tools. For now we take these two facts on faith, and note that the simulation in Figure 2 is a direct check that the end result is right.

Combine into a ttt. A standard normal divided by the square root of an independent chi-square over its degrees of freedom is, by definition, a ttt random variable:

b1−β1s{b1}=(b1−β1)/(σ/Sxx)MSE/σ2=Zχn−22/(n−2)∼tn−2.\frac{b_1 - \beta_1}{s\{b_1\}} = \frac{(b_1 - \beta_1)\big/\big(\sigma/\sqrt{S_{xx}}\big)}{\sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}/\sigma^2}} = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{\chi^2_{n-2}/(n-2)}} \sim t_{n-2}.s{b1​}b1​−β1​​=MSE/σ2​(b1​−β1​)/(σ/Sxx​​)​=χn−22​/(n−2)​Z​∼tn−2​.

The middle step just multiplies top and bottom out: the numerator is ZZZ, and s{b1}/(σ/Sxx)=MSE/σ2=χn−22/(n−2)s\{b_1\}/(\sigma/\sqrt{S_{xx}}) = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}/\sigma^2} = \sqrt{\chi^2_{n-2}/(n-2)}s{b1​}/(σ/Sxx​​)=MSE/σ2​=χn−22​/(n−2)​. The identical argument with the weights mim_imi​ from 2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem gives the ttt statistic for b0b_0b0​. ■\blacksquare■

The setup code below refits the Toluca model and recovers the standard errors we will use throughout.

toluca <- read.csv("data/toluca.csv")
fit <- lm(hours ~ lotsize, data = toluca)
n <- nrow(toluca)
x <- toluca$lotsize
y <- toluca$hours
Sxx <- sum((x - mean(x))^2)
MSE <- sum(residuals(fit)^2) / (n - 2)
round(c(n = n, Sxx = Sxx, MSE = MSE, s = sqrt(MSE),
        se_b1 = sqrt(MSE / Sxx)), 4)
         n        Sxx        MSE          s      se_b1 
   25.0000 19800.0000  2383.7156    48.8233     0.3470 
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

toluca = pd.read_csv("data/toluca.csv")
fit = smf.ols("hours ~ lotsize", data=toluca).fit()
n = len(toluca)
x = toluca["lotsize"].to_numpy()
y = toluca["hours"].to_numpy()
Sxx = np.sum((x - x.mean()) ** 2)
MSE = np.sum(fit.resid ** 2) / (n - 2)
print(round(MSE, 4), round(np.sqrt(MSE), 4), round(np.sqrt(MSE / Sxx), 4))
2383.7156 48.8233 0.347

The estimated standard error of the slope is s{b1}=0.347s\{b_1\} = 0.347s{b1​}=0.347 hours per unit, the same number Chapter 2 computed from the variance formula. It is the yardstick for everything that follows.

Key idea

If we knew σ\sigmaσ, the standardized slope would be exactly standard normal. We do not: we estimate it with s=MSEs = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}s=MSE​. That single substitution injects extra uncertainty and is the whole reason inference here uses a ttt distribution with n−2n-2n−2 degrees of freedom rather than the normal. As nnn grows and sss steadies, the ttt shrinks back toward the normal.

Try it 3.1

The standardized slope follows a t23t_{23}t23​ distribution, not a standard normal. For a 95% interval, the normal multiplier is 1.96. Look up the t23t_{23}t23​ multiplier (in R, qt(0.975, 23)). Is it larger or smaller than 1.96, and why does that direction make sense?

Solution

The multiplier is t0.975,23=2.069t_{0.975, 23} = 2.069t0.975,23​=2.069, larger than 1.96. It has to be larger: replacing the known σ\sigmaσ with the estimate sss injects extra uncertainty, so the ttt distribution has heavier tails than the normal, and a wider multiplier is needed to still capture 95% of the distribution. As nnn grows, the estimate sss becomes reliable and tn−2t_{n-2}tn−2​ approaches the normal, so the multiplier shrinks toward 1.96.

3.2 Inference for the slope¶

Intuition¶

The slope is usually the number a study is about: does lot size affect hours, and by how much? Two questions come in a pair. A confidence interval (Definition 3.3) answers “how much?” by giving a range of plausible values for β1\beta_1β1​. A hypothesis test answers “is there any effect at all?” by asking whether zero is a plausible value. Both are built from the one ttt statistic of 3.1 The sampling distributions of b0b_0b0​ and b1b_1b1​, so they always agree: a 95% interval excludes zero exactly when the two-sided test rejects at the 5% level.

Formula¶

Definition 3.3: Confidence interval for the slope

A 100(1−α)%100(1-\alpha)\%100(1−α)% confidence interval for the slope β1\beta_1β1​ is

b1±t1−α/2, n−2  s{b1},s{b1}=MSE/Sxx,b_1 \pm t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}\; s\{b_1\}, \qquad s\{b_1\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}/S_{xx}},b1​±t1−α/2,n−2​s{b1​},s{b1​}=MSE/Sxx​​,

the estimate reaching out t1−α/2, n−2t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}t1−α/2,n−2​ estimated standard errors on each side. It covers the true slope in 100(1−α)%100(1-\alpha)\%100(1−α)% of repeated samples.

In words: take the estimate and reach out a multiplier’s worth of standard errors on each side. To test H0:β1=0H_0: \beta_1 = 0H0​:β1​=0 against Ha:β1≠0H_a: \beta_1 \ne 0Ha​:β1​=0, form the test statistic

t∗=b1−0s{b1},t^{\ast} = \frac{b_1 - 0}{s\{b_1\}},t∗=s{b1​}b1​−0​,

and reject H0H_0H0​ when ∣t∗∣>t1−α/2, n−2|t^{\ast}| > t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}∣t∗∣>t1−α/2,n−2​, or equivalently when the two-sided p-value 2 P(tn−2>∣t∗∣)2\,P(t_{n-2} > |t^{\ast}|)2P(tn−2​>∣t∗∣) is below α\alphaα. In words: if the estimate sits many standard errors away from zero, zero is not credible. Nothing here is special to zero; to test H0:β1=cH_0: \beta_1 = cH0​:β1​=c for any value ccc, put ccc in place of the 0.

Example 3.1: Is lot size related to hours, and by how much?

Question. Test whether the Toluca slope differs from zero at the 5% level, and give a 95% confidence interval for it.

Intuition. The estimate 3.57 is about ten standard errors of 0.347 away from zero, so zero will not survive. The interval reaches out t0.975,23=2.069t_{0.975, 23} = 2.069t0.975,23​=2.069 standard errors each way.

Formula. t∗=b1/s{b1}t^{\ast} = b_1/s\{b_1\}t∗=b1​/s{b1​} and b1±t0.975,23 s{b1}b_1 \pm t_{0.975, 23}\, s\{b_1\}b1​±t0.975,23​s{b1​}.

Computation.

summary(fit)$coefficients
confint(fit, "lotsize", level = 0.95)
             Estimate Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 62.365859 26.1774339  2.382428 2.585094e-02
lotsize      3.570202  0.3469722 10.289592 4.448828e-10
           2.5 %   97.5 %
lotsize 2.852435 4.287969
print(fit.summary().tables[1])
print(fit.conf_int(alpha=0.05).loc["lotsize"])
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept     62.3659     26.177      2.382      0.026       8.214     116.518
lotsize        3.5702      0.347     10.290      0.000       2.852       4.288
==============================================================================
0    2.852435
1    4.287969
Name: lotsize, dtype: float64

Interpretation. The test statistic is t∗=10.29t^{\ast} = 10.29t∗=10.29, far beyond the critical value 2.069, with a two-sided p-value of 4.45×10−104.45 \times 10^{-10}4.45×10−10. We reject H0:β1=0H_0: \beta_1 = 0H0​:β1​=0: lot size is strongly related to work hours. The 95% confidence interval is (2.85,4.29)(2.85, 4.29)(2.85,4.29) hours per unit. So a plausible range for the true effect of one more unit per lot is roughly 2.9 to 4.3 hours, and the whole interval sits well above zero. That tiny p-value is the number Chapter 5 will compare against a permutation test that assumes nothing about normality (5.2 The permutation test for the slope).

Durable skill: Report an estimate with its uncertainty

An estimate alone (b1=3.57b_1 = 3.57b1​=3.57) invites false confidence; an estimate with an interval (3.57, 95% CI 2.85 to 4.29) tells the reader how firmly the data pin it down. Whenever you report a number that came from data, report a range with it, and say what the range means. This habit separates an analyst from a calculator, and it is what a decision-maker actually needs. To practice, never write a slope, mean, or proportion in a report without the interval beside it.

Try it 3.2

A colleague tests H0:β1=3H_0: \beta_1 = 3H0​:β1​=3 (a supplier’s claimed rate of 3 hours per unit) against a two-sided alternative. Using b1=3.5702b_1 = 3.5702b1​=3.5702 and s{b1}=0.3470s\{b_1\} = 0.3470s{b1​}=0.3470, compute t∗t^{\ast}t∗ and decide at the 5% level.

Solution

t∗=(3.5702−3)/0.3470=1.643t^{\ast} = (3.5702 - 3)/0.3470 = 1.643t∗=(3.5702−3)/0.3470=1.643. Compared with t0.975,23=2.069t_{0.975, 23} = 2.069t0.975,23​=2.069, we have ∣t∗∣<2.069|t^{\ast}| < 2.069∣t∗∣<2.069, so we do not reject: a true rate of 3 hours per unit is consistent with the data. Equivalently, 3 lies inside the 95% interval (2.85,4.29)(2.85, 4.29)(2.85,4.29). Notice the same data reject β1=0\beta_1 = 0β1​=0 overwhelmingly but cannot rule out β1=3\beta_1 = 3β1​=3; “significant” always means “significantly different from a specific value,” almost always zero.

3.3 Inference for the intercept¶

The intercept is where the fitted line crosses the vertical axis: the model’s predicted hours for a lot of zero units. It is the same kind of estimate as the slope, so it gets the exact same machinery. Follow the roadmap in Figure 3 one box over, and only the standard error changes.

The intercept gets the same treatment, with its own standard error s{b0}=MSE (1/n+Xˉ2/Sxx)s\{b_0\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}\,(1/n + \bar{X}^2/S_{xx})}s{b0​}=MSE(1/n+Xˉ2/Sxx​)​. The confidence interval is b0±t1−α/2, n−2 s{b0}b_0 \pm t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}\, s\{b_0\}b0​±t1−α/2,n−2​s{b0​}, and the test of H0:β0=0H_0: \beta_0 = 0H0​:β0​=0 uses t∗=b0/s{b0}t^{\ast} = b_0/s\{b_0\}t∗=b0​/s{b0​}.

The reason the intercept comes second is that it is usually less interesting and often not interpretable. For Toluca, β0\beta_0β0​ is the mean hours at a lot size of zero, an extrapolation far outside the data (2.2 Least squares from first principles), so a test of β0=0\beta_0 = 0β0​=0 answers a question nobody asked. The interval is still worth computing, if only to see how loosely the intercept is pinned down.

confint(fit, "(Intercept)", level = 0.95)
               2.5 %  97.5 %
(Intercept) 8.213711 116.518
print(fit.conf_int(alpha=0.05).loc["Intercept"])
0      8.213711
1    116.518006
Name: Intercept, dtype: float64

The 95% interval for the intercept runs from about 8 to 117 hours, a span of over 100 hours. That enormous width is the price of extrapolating to X=0X = 0X=0, which sits 70 units below the smallest observed lot. Compare it with the slope’s tight interval: the slope is anchored by data on both sides, the intercept is not.

Try it 3.3

The intercept’s p-value in the summary table is 0.026, below 0.05. A student concludes “the setup time for a lot of zero units is significantly greater than zero, so there is real fixed cost.” What is wrong with this reading?

Solution

Two things. First, X=0X = 0X=0 is far outside the data (the smallest lot is 20 units), so b0b_0b0​ is an extrapolation and does not estimate a real “setup time.” Second, statistical significance says the estimate is far from zero relative to its standard error; it does not certify that the quantity is physically meaningful. A significant intercept for extrapolated data is a fact about the fitted line’s height, not evidence about hours to make nothing.

3.4 Confidence interval for a mean response¶

Intuition¶

Now we move from the coefficients to a prediction. Ask: across all runs of lot size Xh=65X_h = 65Xh​=65, what is the average number of hours? That average is the point on the true line, E{Yh}=β0+β1XhE\{Y_h\} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_hE{Yh​}=β0​+β1​Xh​, and our estimate of it is the fitted value Y^h=b0+b1Xh\hat{Y}_h = b_0 + b_1 X_hY^h​=b0​+b1​Xh​. Because b0b_0b0​ and b1b_1b1​ wobble from sample to sample, so does Y^h\hat{Y}_hY^h​, and we want an interval for the true mean.

The key feature is that the interval’s width depends on where XhX_hXh​ sits. Near the center Xˉ\bar{X}Xˉ the fitted line is pinned down tightly, so the interval is narrow. Far from Xˉ\bar{X}Xˉ a small tilt in the slope swings the line up or down a lot, so the interval widens. This is the pinch we saw in the bundle of fitted lines in Figure 12, now made into a formula.

Formula¶

The estimated variance of the fitted mean at XhX_hXh​ is

s2{Y^h}=MSE(1n+(Xh−Xˉ)2Sxx),s^2\{\hat{Y}_h\} = \mathrm{MSE}\left(\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_h - \bar{X})^2}{S_{xx}}\right),s2{Y^h​}=MSE(n1​+Sxx​(Xh​−Xˉ)2​),

and the confidence interval for the mean response follows.

Definition 3.4: Confidence interval for a mean response

A 100(1−α)%100(1-\alpha)\%100(1−α)% confidence interval for the mean response E{Yh}=β0+β1XhE\{Y_h\} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_hE{Yh​}=β0​+β1​Xh​ at a given predictor value XhX_hXh​ is

Y^h±t1−α/2, n−2  s{Y^h},s2{Y^h}=MSE(1n+(Xh−Xˉ)2Sxx).\hat{Y}_h \pm t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}\; s\{\hat{Y}_h\}, \qquad s^2\{\hat{Y}_h\} = \mathrm{MSE}\left(\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_h - \bar{X})^2}{S_{xx}}\right).Y^h​±t1−α/2,n−2​s{Y^h​},s2{Y^h​}=MSE(n1​+Sxx​(Xh​−Xˉ)2​).

It is narrowest at Xh=XˉX_h = \bar{X}Xh​=Xˉ and widens as XhX_hXh​ moves away.

In words: the further XhX_hXh​ is from the average predictor value Xˉ\bar{X}Xˉ, the larger the second term inside the parentheses, and the wider the interval. At Xh=XˉX_h = \bar{X}Xh​=Xˉ the second term vanishes and the interval is at its narrowest, width set only by 1/n1/n1/n.

Derivation¶

Theorem 3.5: Variance of the fitted mean

Under the SLR model, the fitted value Y^h=b0+b1Xh\hat{Y}_h = b_0 + b_1 X_hY^h​=b0​+b1​Xh​ at a fixed XhX_hXh​ has variance

Var⁡{Y^h}=σ2 ⁣(1n+(Xh−Xˉ)2Sxx),\operatorname{Var}\{\hat{Y}_h\} = \sigma^2\!\left(\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_h - \bar{X})^2}{S_{xx}}\right),Var{Y^h​}=σ2(n1​+Sxx​(Xh​−Xˉ)2​),

which the estimate s2{Y^h}s^2\{\hat{Y}_h\}s2{Y^h​} obtains by replacing σ2\sigma^2σ2 with MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE.

Derivation (variance of the fitted mean). Write the fitted value in its pivoted form, using b0=Yˉ−b1Xˉb_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X}b0​=Yˉ−b1​Xˉ:

Y^h=b0+b1Xh=Yˉ+b1(Xh−Xˉ).\hat{Y}_h = b_0 + b_1 X_h = \bar{Y} + b_1 (X_h - \bar{X}).Y^h​=b0​+b1​Xh​=Yˉ+b1​(Xh​−Xˉ).

The two pieces Yˉ\bar{Y}Yˉ and b1b_1b1​ are uncorrelated. To see it, recall b1=∑kiYib_1 = \sum k_i Y_ib1​=∑ki​Yi​ and Yˉ=∑1nYi\bar{Y} = \sum \tfrac{1}{n} Y_iYˉ=∑n1​Yi​, so, using independence of the YiY_iYi​ and ∑ki=0\sum k_i = 0∑ki​=0,

Cov⁡{Yˉ,b1}=∑i1n kiVar⁡{Yi}=σ2n∑iki=0.\operatorname{Cov}\{\bar{Y}, b_1\} = \sum_i \frac{1}{n}\, k_i \operatorname{Var}\{Y_i\} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \sum_i k_i = 0.Cov{Yˉ,b1​}=i∑​n1​ki​Var{Yi​}=nσ2​i∑​ki​=0.

Because the two pieces are uncorrelated, their variances add:

Var⁡{Y^h}=Var⁡{Yˉ}+(Xh−Xˉ)2Var⁡{b1}=σ2n+(Xh−Xˉ)2σ2Sxx=σ2 ⁣(1n+(Xh−Xˉ)2Sxx).\operatorname{Var}\{\hat{Y}_h\} = \operatorname{Var}\{\bar{Y}\} + (X_h - \bar{X})^2 \operatorname{Var}\{b_1\} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} + (X_h - \bar{X})^2 \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}} = \sigma^2\!\left(\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_h - \bar{X})^2}{S_{xx}}\right).Var{Y^h​}=Var{Yˉ}+(Xh​−Xˉ)2Var{b1​}=nσ2​+(Xh​−Xˉ)2Sxx​σ2​=σ2(n1​+Sxx​(Xh​−Xˉ)2​).

Replacing σ2\sigma^2σ2 by MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE gives s2{Y^h}s^2\{\hat{Y}_h\}s2{Y^h​}. The ttt interval then follows from the same standardize-and-divide argument as in 3.1 The sampling distributions of b0b_0b0​ and b1b_1b1​, since (Y^h−E{Yh})/s{Y^h}∼tn−2(\hat{Y}_h - E\{Y_h\})/s\{\hat{Y}_h\} \sim t_{n-2}(Y^h​−E{Yh​})/s{Y^h​}∼tn−2​. ■\blacksquare■

Figure 4 plots s{Y^h}s\{\hat{Y}_h\}s{Y^h​} against XhX_hXh​: a curve that dips to its minimum at Xˉ=70\bar{X} = 70Xˉ=70 and rises symmetrically on both sides. That curve is what gives the confidence band in Figure 1 its gentle bow-tie shape.

Example 3.2: Mean hours for lots of 65 units

Question. Give a 95% confidence interval for the average work hours across all runs of lot size 65.

Intuition. The point estimate is the fitted value Y^(65)=294.4\hat{Y}(65) = 294.4Y^(65)=294.4 from 2.2 Least squares from first principles. Since 65 is close to Xˉ=70\bar{X} = 70Xˉ=70, the interval should be fairly narrow.

Formula. Y^h±t0.975,23 s{Y^h}\hat{Y}_h \pm t_{0.975, 23}\, s\{\hat{Y}_h\}Y^h​±t0.975,23​s{Y^h​} with s{Y^h}=MSE(1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)s\{\hat{Y}_h\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}(1/n + (X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx})}s{Y^h​}=MSE(1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​)​.

Computation.

predict(fit, newdata = data.frame(lotsize = 65), interval = "confidence")
      fit      lwr      upr
1 294.429 273.9129 314.9451
pr = fit.get_prediction(pd.DataFrame({"lotsize": [65]}))
print(pr.predicted_mean, pr.conf_int(alpha=0.05))
[294.4289899] [[273.91291531 314.94506449]]

Interpretation. We estimate the mean hours for 65-unit lots at 294.4, and we are 95% confident the true mean lies between 274 and 315 hours. The interval is about ±20\pm 20±20 hours wide because 65 is near the center of the data, where the line is best determined.

Try it 3.4

Without running code, would a 95% confidence interval for the mean hours at Xh=30X_h = 30Xh​=30 units be narrower or wider than the one at Xh=65X_h = 65Xh​=65? Why?

Solution

Wider. The width grows with (Xh−Xˉ)2(X_h - \bar{X})^2(Xh​−Xˉ)2. At Xh=30X_h = 30Xh​=30 the distance from Xˉ=70\bar{X} = 70Xˉ=70 is 40 units, versus only 5 units at Xh=65X_h = 65Xh​=65, so (Xh−Xˉ)2(X_h - \bar{X})^2(Xh​−Xˉ)2 is 1600 versus 25. The fitted line is much less certain out near 30, so the mean-response interval there is noticeably wider. This is the rising left arm of the curve in Figure 4.

3.5 Prediction interval for a new observation¶

Intuition¶

Here is the distinction that trips up more practitioners than any other. The confidence interval of 3.4 Confidence interval for a mean response is about the average run of size XhX_hXh​. But the engineers do not schedule an average run; they schedule one specific run next week, and they want to know where its hours will land. A single run scatters around the mean by its own random error ε\varepsilonε, and no amount of data removes that scatter. So predicting a single new observation carries two sources of uncertainty: our uncertainty about where the line is (the same s2{Y^h}s^2\{\hat{Y}_h\}s2{Y^h​} as before), plus the new run’s own irreducible variance σ2\sigma^2σ2. The interval that accounts for both is the prediction interval (Definition 3.6), and it is always wider than the confidence interval.

A common wrong reading, corrected

A manager sees the mean-response interval (274,315)(274, 315)(274,315) for lot size 65 and budgets a specific new 65-unit run at “somewhere between 274 and 315 hours, 95% sure.” That is wrong, and it will blow the budget. That interval is for the average of many runs; a single run scatters far more. The right interval for one new run at Xh=65X_h = 65Xh​=65 is the prediction interval (191,397)(191, 397)(191,397), more than twice as wide. Use the confidence interval to describe the process on average; use the prediction interval to plan for one case.

The whole choice comes down to one question, and Figure 5 lays it out. Ask what you are bounding: the average outcome over many cases at XhX_hXh​, or the outcome of one specific new case. The left answer is a confidence interval; the right answer is a prediction interval. Decide the question first, and the formula follows.

Formula¶

For a new observation Yh(new)Y_{h(\text{new})}Yh(new)​ at XhX_hXh​, the estimated variance of the prediction error is s2{pred}=MSE(1+1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)s^2\{\text{pred}\} = \mathrm{MSE}(1 + 1/n + (X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx})s2{pred}=MSE(1+1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​), and the prediction interval follows.

Definition 3.6: Prediction interval for a new observation

A 100(1−α)%100(1-\alpha)\%100(1−α)% prediction interval for a single new observation Yh(new)Y_{h(\text{new})}Yh(new)​ at XhX_hXh​ is

Y^h±t1−α/2, n−2  s{pred},s2{pred}=MSE(1+1n+(Xh−Xˉ)2Sxx).\hat{Y}_h \pm t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}\; s\{\text{pred}\}, \qquad s^2\{\text{pred}\} = \mathrm{MSE}\left(1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_h - \bar{X})^2}{S_{xx}}\right).Y^h​±t1−α/2,n−2​s{pred},s2{pred}=MSE(1+n1​+Sxx​(Xh​−Xˉ)2​).

The leading 1 inside the parentheses is the new run’s own scatter, absent from the mean-response interval, so the prediction interval is always wider.

In words: this is the mean-response variance with an extra MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE (the leading 1) added for the new run’s own scatter. That extra term is usually the dominant one, which is why the prediction interval is much wider and why, unlike the confidence interval, it never shrinks to zero even with infinite data.

Derivation¶

Theorem 3.7: Variance of a prediction

Under the SLR model, the prediction error Yh(new)−Y^hY_{h(\text{new})} - \hat{Y}_hYh(new)​−Y^h​ for a new observation at XhX_hXh​, whose error εnew\varepsilon_{\text{new}}εnew​ is independent of the fitting data, has variance

Var⁡{Yh(new)−Y^h}=σ2 ⁣(1+1n+(Xh−Xˉ)2Sxx),\operatorname{Var}\{Y_{h(\text{new})} - \hat{Y}_h\} = \sigma^2\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_h - \bar{X})^2}{S_{xx}}\right),Var{Yh(new)​−Y^h​}=σ2(1+n1​+Sxx​(Xh​−Xˉ)2​),

which the estimate s2{pred}s^2\{\text{pred}\}s2{pred} obtains by replacing σ2\sigma^2σ2 with MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE.

Derivation (variance of a prediction). The quantity we bound is the prediction error Yh(new)−Y^hY_{h(\text{new})} - \hat{Y}_hYh(new)​−Y^h​, the gap between the actual new run and our forecast. The new run is Yh(new)=β0+β1Xh+εnewY_{h(\text{new})} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_h + \varepsilon_{\text{new}}Yh(new)​=β0​+β1​Xh​+εnew​, and its error εnew\varepsilon_{\text{new}}εnew​ is independent of the past data that produced Y^h\hat{Y}_hY^h​. Two independent pieces, so their variances add:

Var⁡{Yh(new)−Y^h}=Var⁡{εnew}⏟σ2+Var⁡{Y^h}⏟σ2(1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)=σ2 ⁣(1+1n+(Xh−Xˉ)2Sxx).\operatorname{Var}\{Y_{h(\text{new})} - \hat{Y}_h\} = \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}\{\varepsilon_{\text{new}}\}}_{\sigma^2} + \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}\{\hat{Y}_h\}}_{\sigma^2(1/n + (X_h - \bar X)^2/S_{xx})} = \sigma^2\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_h - \bar{X})^2}{S_{xx}}\right).Var{Yh(new)​−Y^h​}=σ2Var{εnew​}​​+σ2(1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​)Var{Y^h​}​​=σ2(1+n1​+Sxx​(Xh​−Xˉ)2​).

Replacing σ2\sigma^2σ2 by MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE gives s2{pred}s^2\{\text{pred}\}s2{pred}, and standardizing the prediction error by s{pred}s\{\text{pred}\}s{pred} again yields a tn−2t_{n-2}tn−2​ distribution. The ttt interval follows. ■\blacksquare■

Key idea

A confidence interval and a prediction interval answer two different questions, not one question at two confidence levels. The confidence interval bounds the average response at XhX_hXh​; the prediction interval bounds a single new response, so it carries the new case’s own error σ2\sigma^2σ2 on top. That extra term never vanishes, even with infinite data, which is why the prediction interval stays wide. Decide which question you are asking before you compute anything.

Example 3.3: Budgeting a single new run of 100 units

Question. Next week’s run is 100 units. Give a 95% interval for the hours that specific run will take, and contrast it with the interval for the mean hours of all 100-unit runs.

Intuition. Both are centered at Y^(100)=419.4\hat{Y}(100) = 419.4Y^(100)=419.4. The mean-response interval is narrow; the prediction interval adds the run’s own scatter and is much wider.

Formula. Mean: Y^h±t s{Y^h}\hat{Y}_h \pm t\, s\{\hat{Y}_h\}Y^h​±ts{Y^h​}. Prediction: Y^h±t s{pred}\hat{Y}_h \pm t\, s\{\text{pred}\}Y^h​±ts{pred}.

Computation.

predict(fit, newdata = data.frame(lotsize = 100), interval = "confidence")
predict(fit, newdata = data.frame(lotsize = 100), interval = "prediction")
       fit      lwr      upr
1 419.3861 389.8615 448.9106
       fit      lwr      upr
1 419.3861 314.1604 524.6117
pr = fit.get_prediction(pd.DataFrame({"lotsize": [100]}))
print("mean CI ", pr.conf_int(alpha=0.05))
print("pred PI ", pr.conf_int(obs=True, alpha=0.05))
mean CI  [[389.86150154 448.91061967]]
pred PI  [[314.16040096 524.61172025]]

Interpretation. The mean of all 100-unit runs is estimated at 419 hours, 95% interval (390,449)(390, 449)(390,449), a span of about 60 hours. But next week’s single run has a 95% prediction interval of (314,525)(314, 525)(314,525), a span of about 210 hours, more than three times wider. To budget the one run honestly, use the prediction interval: plan for as few as 314 and as many as 525 hours. The mean-response interval, used here, would badly understate the risk.

The gap between the two intervals is not a modeling choice; it is a fact about the two questions, and each interval keeps its own 95% promise. We can check this by simulation, which is the surest way to see that a prediction interval really does trap a new observation 95% of the time. Figure 6 runs the experiment.

set.seed(4210)
b0_true <- 62.366; b1_true <- 3.5702; sigma <- 48.82
xh <- 100
mu_h <- b0_true + b1_true * xh
tcrit <- qt(0.975, n - 2)
ci_hits <- 0; pi_hits <- 0; N <- 5000
for (i in 1:N) {
  ysim <- b0_true + b1_true * x + rnorm(n, 0, sigma)
  f <- lm(ysim ~ x)
  pr <- predict(f, data.frame(x = xh), se.fit = TRUE)
  s_mean <- pr$se.fit
  s_pred <- sqrt(pr$se.fit^2 + sum(residuals(f)^2) / (n - 2))
  if (abs(pr$fit - mu_h) <= tcrit * s_mean) ci_hits <- ci_hits + 1
  ynew <- mu_h + rnorm(1, 0, sigma)
  if (abs(pr$fit - ynew) <= tcrit * s_pred) pi_hits <- pi_hits + 1
}
round(c(CI_covers_mean = ci_hits / N, PI_covers_new = pi_hits / N), 4)
CI_covers_mean  PI_covers_new 
        0.9496         0.9458 
from scipy import stats

rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
b0_true, b1_true, sigma = 62.366, 3.5702, 48.82
xh = 100.0
mu_h = b0_true + b1_true * xh
tcrit = stats.t.ppf(0.975, n - 2)
ci_hits = pi_hits = 0
N = 5000
for _ in range(N):
    ysim = b0_true + b1_true * x + rng.normal(0, sigma, size=n)
    b1s = np.sum((x - x.mean()) * (ysim - ysim.mean())) / Sxx
    b0s = ysim.mean() - b1s * x.mean()
    yhat = b0s + b1s * xh
    mse = np.sum((ysim - (b0s + b1s * x)) ** 2) / (n - 2)
    s_mean = np.sqrt(mse * (1 / n + (xh - x.mean()) ** 2 / Sxx))
    s_pred = np.sqrt(mse * (1 + 1 / n + (xh - x.mean()) ** 2 / Sxx))
    if abs(yhat - mu_h) <= tcrit * s_mean:
        ci_hits += 1
    ynew = mu_h + rng.normal(0, sigma)
    if abs(yhat - ynew) <= tcrit * s_pred:
        pi_hits += 1
print(round(ci_hits / N, 4), round(pi_hits / N, 4))
0.9492 0.9544

Across 5000 simulated datasets, the confidence interval covers the true mean about 95% of the time and the prediction interval covers a fresh new run about 95% of the time. Each interval is honest about its own question. The prediction interval earns its extra width; it is not being cautious for the sake of caution.

Durable skill: Match the interval to the question

Before you compute any interval, decide what you are asking about: a mean over many cases or a single new case. “What is the typical delivery time for orders like this?” wants a confidence interval; “how long will this order take?” wants a prediction interval. The wrong choice is not a rounding error, it can be a factor of three or more in width, as the Toluca run shows. State the question in words first, then pick the interval. This discipline carries into forecasting, quality control, and any planning under uncertainty.

3.6 The analysis of variance approach¶

Intuition¶

There is a second, equivalent way to see the same inference, and it scales up beautifully to the multiple regression of later chapters. The idea is bookkeeping for variation. The response values YiY_iYi​ vary; some of that variation the line explains, and some it leaves as residual scatter. If we can split the total variation cleanly into an “explained” part and a “leftover” part, we can compare them, and a big explained part relative to leftover is evidence that the slope is real.

Figure 7 shows the split for a single run: the total deviation from the mean, Yi−YˉY_i - \bar{Y}Yi​−Yˉ, breaks into the part the line accounts for, Y^i−Yˉ\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y}Y^i​−Yˉ, plus the residual, Yi−Y^iY_i - \hat{Y}_iYi​−Y^i​. Remarkably, when you square these and sum over all runs, the cross term vanishes and the totals split just as cleanly.

Formula¶

Define the three sums of squares:

∑i=1n(Yi−Yˉ)2⏟SSTO  =  ∑i=1n(Y^i−Yˉ)2⏟SSR  +  ∑i=1n(Yi−Y^i)2⏟SSE.\underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}_{\mathrm{SSTO}} \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2}_{\mathrm{SSR}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2}_{\mathrm{SSE}} .SSTOi=1∑n​(Yi​−Yˉ)2​​=SSRi=1∑n​(Y^i​−Yˉ)2​​+SSEi=1∑n​(Yi​−Y^i​)2​​.

In words: the total variation of YYY about its mean (SSTO) equals the variation the regression explains (SSR, the spread of the fitted values about the mean) plus the variation it does not (SSE, the error sum of squares from 2.4 Estimating the error variance). Each sum has degrees of freedom: n−1n-1n−1 for SSTO, 1 for SSR (one predictor), and n−2n-2n−2 for SSE. The degrees of freedom add the same way, n−1=1+(n−2)n-1 = 1 + (n-2)n−1=1+(n−2). Dividing each sum of squares by its degrees of freedom gives a mean square (Definition 3.8).

Definition 3.8: Mean square

A mean square is a sum of squares divided by its degrees of freedom. The regression and error mean squares are

MSR=SSR1,MSE=SSEn−2.\mathrm{MSR} = \frac{\mathrm{SSR}}{1}, \qquad \mathrm{MSE} = \frac{\mathrm{SSE}}{n-2}.MSR=1SSR​,MSE=n−2SSE​.

In words: a mean square is a sum of squares averaged over its degrees of freedom, so it is a variance-like number. MSR and MSE are the two we will put in a ratio to test the slope.

Derivation¶

Theorem 3.9: The ANOVA decomposition

Under the SLR model, the total variation of the response splits exactly into an explained part and a residual part,

∑i=1n(Yi−Yˉ)2⏟SSTO=∑i=1n(Y^i−Yˉ)2⏟SSR+∑i=1n(Yi−Y^i)2⏟SSE,\underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}_{\mathrm{SSTO}} = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2}_{\mathrm{SSR}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2}_{\mathrm{SSE}},SSTOi=1∑n​(Yi​−Yˉ)2​​=SSRi=1∑n​(Y^i​−Yˉ)2​​+SSEi=1∑n​(Yi​−Y^i​)2​​,

with degrees of freedom adding the same way, n−1=1+(n−2)n-1 = 1 + (n-2)n−1=1+(n−2).

Derivation (SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE). Split each total deviation by routing it through the fitted value:

Yi−Yˉ=(Y^i−Yˉ)+(Yi−Y^i).Y_i - \bar{Y} = (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y}) + (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i).Yi​−Yˉ=(Y^i​−Yˉ)+(Yi​−Y^i​).

Square both sides and sum over iii:

∑(Yi−Yˉ)2=∑(Y^i−Yˉ)2+∑(Yi−Y^i)2+2∑(Y^i−Yˉ)(Yi−Y^i).\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = \sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2 + \sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2 + 2\sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})(Y_i - \hat{Y}_i).∑(Yi​−Yˉ)2=∑(Y^i​−Yˉ)2+∑(Yi​−Y^i​)2+2∑(Y^i​−Yˉ)(Yi​−Y^i​).

The cross term is zero. Write Yi−Y^i=eiY_i - \hat{Y}_i = e_iYi​−Y^i​=ei​ and expand:

∑(Y^i−Yˉ)ei=∑Y^iei−Yˉ∑ei.\sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y}) e_i = \sum \hat{Y}_i e_i - \bar{Y} \sum e_i.∑(Y^i​−Yˉ)ei​=∑Y^i​ei​−Yˉ∑ei​.

Both pieces vanish by the residual identities of 2.3 Fitted values and the properties of residuals: ∑Y^iei=0\sum \hat{Y}_i e_i = 0∑Y^i​ei​=0 (fitted values orthogonal to residuals) and ∑ei=0\sum e_i = 0∑ei​=0. So the cross term is zero and SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE. The residual identities we proved in Chapter 2 are doing the work again. ■\blacksquare■

Theorem 3.10: Expected mean squares

Under the SLR model,

E{MSE}=σ2,E{MSR}=σ2+β12Sxx.E\{\mathrm{MSE}\} = \sigma^2, \qquad E\{\mathrm{MSR}\} = \sigma^2 + \beta_1^2 S_{xx}.E{MSE}=σ2,E{MSR}=σ2+β12​Sxx​.

MSR exceeds σ2\sigma^2σ2 in expectation exactly when β1≠0\beta_1 \ne 0β1​=0, which is what makes the ratio MSR/MSE\mathrm{MSR}/\mathrm{MSE}MSR/MSE a test for the slope.

Derivation (expected mean squares). From 2.4 Estimating the error variance we already have E{MSE}=σ2E\{\mathrm{MSE}\} = \sigma^2E{MSE}=σ2. A similar expectation calculation, using SSR=b12Sxx\mathrm{SSR} = b_1^2 S_{xx}SSR=b12​Sxx​ and E{b12}=Var⁡{b1}+β12=σ2/Sxx+β12E\{b_1^2\} = \operatorname{Var}\{b_1\} + \beta_1^2 = \sigma^2/S_{xx} + \beta_1^2E{b12​}=Var{b1​}+β12​=σ2/Sxx​+β12​, gives

E{MSR}=E{b12Sxx}=σ2+β12Sxx.E\{\mathrm{MSR}\} = E\{b_1^2 S_{xx}\} = \sigma^2 + \beta_1^2 S_{xx}.E{MSR}=E{b12​Sxx​}=σ2+β12​Sxx​.

In words: MSE always estimates σ2\sigma^2σ2, while MSR estimates σ2\sigma^2σ2 plus a term that is positive exactly when β1≠0\beta_1 \ne 0β1​=0. So if the slope is zero, MSR and MSE estimate the same thing and their ratio should be near 1; if the slope is nonzero, MSR is inflated and the ratio is large. That ratio is the FFF test. ■\blacksquare■

The ANOVA table collects these quantities in a standard layout. This is the anchor later chapters extend to extra sums of squares (8.3 Extra sums of squares).

Example 3.4: The Toluca ANOVA table

Question. Partition the total variation in work hours and read off the mean squares.

Intuition. Software fills the table directly: a regression row (1 df) and a residual row (n−2=23n-2 = 23n−2=23 df), summing to the total.

Formula. SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE, with MSR=SSR/1\mathrm{MSR} = \mathrm{SSR}/1MSR=SSR/1 and MSE=SSE/23\mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/23MSE=SSE/23.

Computation.

anova(fit)
Analysis of Variance Table

Response: hours
          Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
lotsize    1 252378  252378  105.88 4.449e-10 ***
Residuals 23  54825    2384                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
import statsmodels.api as sm

print(sm.stats.anova_lm(fit))
            df         sum_sq        mean_sq           F        PR(>F)
lotsize    1.0  252377.580808  252377.580808  105.875709  4.448828e-10
Residual  23.0   54825.459192    2383.715617           NaN           NaN

Interpretation. The regression explains SSR=252,378\mathrm{SSR} = 252{,}378SSR=252,378 of the total SSTO=252,378+54,825=307,203\mathrm{SSTO} = 252{,}378 + 54{,}825 = 307{,}203SSTO=252,378+54,825=307,203 hours-squared of variation, leaving SSE=54,825\mathrm{SSE} = 54{,}825SSE=54,825 as residual. The mean squares are MSR=252,378\mathrm{MSR} = 252{,}378MSR=252,378 and MSE=2384\mathrm{MSE} = 2384MSE=2384, the same MSE as in Chapter 2. Their ratio, F=105.9F = 105.9F=105.9, is the subject of the next section.

3.7 The F test and its equivalence to the t test¶

Intuition¶

The ANOVA table hands us a test almost for free. Under H0:β1=0H_0: \beta_1 = 0H0​:β1​=0, both MSR and MSE estimate the same σ2\sigma^2σ2, so their ratio F∗=MSR/MSEF^{\ast} = \mathrm{MSR}/\mathrm{MSE}F∗=MSR/MSE hovers near 1. Under Ha:β1≠0H_a: \beta_1 \ne 0Ha​:β1​=0, MSR is inflated by β12Sxx\beta_1^2 S_{xx}β12​Sxx​ and the ratio grows. A large F∗F^{\ast}F∗ is evidence against the null.

But we already tested β1=0\beta_1 = 0β1​=0 with a ttt test in 3.2 Inference for the slope and got t∗=10.29t^{\ast} = 10.29t∗=10.29. Do the two tests agree? They do, exactly and always: in simple linear regression the FFF statistic is the square of the ttt statistic. This is worth proving, because it shows the ANOVA FFF test is not a new test, just the ttt test wearing different clothes.

Formula¶

The FFF test of H0:β1=0H_0: \beta_1 = 0H0​:β1​=0 uses

F∗=MSRMSE∼F1, n−2 under H0,F^{\ast} = \frac{\mathrm{MSR}}{\mathrm{MSE}} \sim F_{1,\,n-2} \text{ under } H_0,F∗=MSEMSR​∼F1,n−2​ under H0​,

and rejects H0H_0H0​ when F∗>F1−α; 1, n−2F^{\ast} > F_{1-\alpha;\,1,\,n-2}F∗>F1−α;1,n−2​. The claimed identity is

F∗=(t∗)2,F^{\ast} = (t^{\ast})^2,F∗=(t∗)2,

where t∗=b1/s{b1}t^{\ast} = b_1/s\{b_1\}t∗=b1​/s{b1​} is the slope ttt statistic. In words: the FFF statistic and the squared ttt statistic are the same number, and the tests give identical p-values.

Derivation¶

Theorem 3.11: The FFF test and its equivalence to the ttt test

Under the SLR model, the ANOVA FFF statistic tests H0:β1=0H_0: \beta_1 = 0H0​:β1​=0 with

F∗=MSRMSE∼F1, n−2 under H0,F^{\ast} = \frac{\mathrm{MSR}}{\mathrm{MSE}} \sim F_{1,\,n-2} \text{ under } H_0,F∗=MSEMSR​∼F1,n−2​ under H0​,

and in simple linear regression it equals the square of the slope ttt statistic,

F∗=(t∗)2,t∗=b1/s{b1},F^{\ast} = (t^{\ast})^2, \qquad t^{\ast} = b_1/s\{b_1\},F∗=(t∗)2,t∗=b1​/s{b1​},

so the two tests give identical p-values, with F1−α; 1, n−2=(t1−α/2, n−2)2F_{1-\alpha;\,1,\,n-2} = (t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2})^2F1−α;1,n−2​=(t1−α/2,n−2​)2.

Derivation (F∗=(t∗)2F^{\ast} = (t^{\ast})^2F∗=(t∗)2). Start from the regression sum of squares. Since Y^i−Yˉ=b1(Xi−Xˉ)\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y} = b_1 (X_i - \bar{X})Y^i​−Yˉ=b1​(Xi​−Xˉ),

SSR=∑(Y^i−Yˉ)2=b12∑(Xi−Xˉ)2=b12Sxx.\mathrm{SSR} = \sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2 = b_1^2 \sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 = b_1^2 S_{xx}.SSR=∑(Y^i​−Yˉ)2=b12​∑(Xi​−Xˉ)2=b12​Sxx​.

With one regression degree of freedom, MSR=SSR=b12Sxx\mathrm{MSR} = \mathrm{SSR} = b_1^2 S_{xx}MSR=SSR=b12​Sxx​. Therefore

F∗=MSRMSE=b12SxxMSE.F^{\ast} = \frac{\mathrm{MSR}}{\mathrm{MSE}} = \frac{b_1^2 S_{xx}}{\mathrm{MSE}}.F∗=MSEMSR​=MSEb12​Sxx​​.

Now write the ttt statistic and square it, using s2{b1}=MSE/Sxxs^2\{b_1\} = \mathrm{MSE}/S_{xx}s2{b1​}=MSE/Sxx​:

(t∗)2=(b1s{b1})2=b12MSE/Sxx=b12SxxMSE=F∗.(t^{\ast})^2 = \left(\frac{b_1}{s\{b_1\}}\right)^2 = \frac{b_1^2}{\mathrm{MSE}/S_{xx}} = \frac{b_1^2 S_{xx}}{\mathrm{MSE}} = F^{\ast}.(t∗)2=(s{b1​}b1​​)2=MSE/Sxx​b12​​=MSEb12​Sxx​​=F∗.

The two expressions are literally the same. Distributionally this matches a standard fact: the square of a tn−2t_{n-2}tn−2​ random variable is an F1, n−2F_{1,\,n-2}F1,n−2​ random variable, so squaring the slope ttt statistic lands on the ANOVA FFF statistic, and the critical values match too, F1−α; 1,n−2=(t1−α/2, n−2)2F_{1-\alpha;\,1,n-2} = (t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2})^2F1−α;1,n−2​=(t1−α/2,n−2​)2. ■\blacksquare■

Key idea

The ANOVA table is variance bookkeeping: split the total variation into what the line explains and what it leaves behind, then compare the two. In simple regression the resulting FFF test is not a new test, just the slope ttt test squared. Its real payoff comes in Chapter 8, where the same bookkeeping tests several slopes at once, something a single ttt cannot do.

Figure 8 confirms the distributional half: square 200,000 draws from a t23t_{23}t23​ and the histogram lands on the F1,23F_{1,23}F1,23​ density, with the 5% critical value 4.28 equal to 2.0692.

Example 3.5: Confirming F equals t squared for Toluca

Question. Verify numerically that the Toluca FFF statistic equals the square of the slope ttt statistic.

Intuition. Compute F∗=SSR/MSEF^{\ast} = \mathrm{SSR}/\mathrm{MSE}F∗=SSR/MSE and t∗=b1/s{b1}t^{\ast} = b_1/s\{b_1\}t∗=b1​/s{b1​} and square the second.

Formula. F∗=b12Sxx/MSEF^{\ast} = b_1^2 S_{xx}/\mathrm{MSE}F∗=b12​Sxx​/MSE and (t∗)2=(b1/s{b1})2(t^{\ast})^2 = (b_1/s\{b_1\})^2(t∗)2=(b1​/s{b1​})2.

Computation.

b1 <- coef(fit)[["lotsize"]]
SSR <- sum((fitted(fit) - mean(y))^2)
Fstat <- SSR / MSE
tstat <- b1 / sqrt(MSE / Sxx)
round(c(F = Fstat, t = tstat, t_squared = tstat^2), 4)
        F         t t_squared 
 105.8757   10.2896  105.8757 
SSR = np.sum((fit.fittedvalues - y.mean()) ** 2)
Fstat = SSR / MSE
tstat = fit.params["lotsize"] / np.sqrt(MSE / Sxx)
print(round(Fstat, 4), round(tstat, 4), round(tstat ** 2, 4))
105.8757 10.2896 105.8757

Interpretation. The FFF statistic 105.8757 equals t∗t^{\ast}t∗ squared, 10.28962=105.875710.2896^2 = 105.875710.28962=105.8757, to every digit. The ANOVA FFF test and the slope ttt test are the same test in simple linear regression. The advantage of the FFF form is that it generalizes: in multiple regression the FFF test can test several slopes at once, where a single ttt cannot (8.4 The general linear test).

Try it 3.5

In a simple linear regression, software reports F∗=49.0F^{\ast} = 49.0F∗=49.0 for the slope. Without more information, what is the two-sided ttt statistic for the slope (up to sign)?

Solution

Since F∗=(t∗)2F^{\ast} = (t^{\ast})^2F∗=(t∗)2 in simple linear regression, t∗=±49.0=±7.0t^{\ast} = \pm\sqrt{49.0} = \pm 7.0t∗=±49.0​=±7.0. The sign matches the sign of the slope estimate, which the FFF statistic alone does not reveal (squaring throws it away). This is one reason to report the ttt test for a single slope: it keeps the direction.

3.8 R-squared and its interpretation¶

Intuition¶

The ANOVA split invites a natural summary: what fraction of the total variation did the line explain? That fraction is R2R^2R2, the coefficient of determination (Definition 3.12). It runs from 0 (the line explains nothing beyond the mean) to 1 (the line passes through every point). It is the single most reported and most misread number in regression, so we will state plainly what it does and does not mean.

Formula¶

Definition 3.12: Coefficient of determination

The coefficient of determination R2R^2R2 is the proportion of the total variation in YYY that the regression explains,

R2=SSRSSTO=1−SSESSTO.R^2 = \frac{\mathrm{SSR}}{\mathrm{SSTO}} = 1 - \frac{\mathrm{SSE}}{\mathrm{SSTO}}.R2=SSTOSSR​=1−SSTOSSE​.

In simple linear regression it equals r2r^2r2, the square of the Pearson correlation between XXX and YYY.

In words: R2R^2R2 is the proportion of the total variation in YYY that the regression on XXX accounts for. In simple linear regression it also equals r2r^2r2, the square of the Pearson correlation between XXX and YYY, a link Chapter 4 develops (4.2 Correlation and the regression slope).

Figure 9 shows the two ingredients: the total scatter of hours about their mean (SSTO, left) shrinks to the much smaller residual scatter about the line (SSE, right). R2R^2R2 is how much of the vertical spread the line removed.

Example 3.6: R-squared for Toluca

Question. What fraction of the variation in work hours does lot size explain?

Computation.

SSTO <- sum((y - mean(y))^2)
round(c(SSR = SSR, SSE = sum(residuals(fit)^2), SSTO = SSTO,
        R2 = SSR / SSTO, r = sqrt(SSR / SSTO)), 4)
        SSR         SSE        SSTO          R2           r 
252377.5808  54825.4592 307203.0400      0.8215      0.9064 
SSTO = np.sum((y - y.mean()) ** 2)
print(round(SSR, 4), round(SSTO, 4), round(SSR / SSTO, 4),
      round(np.sqrt(SSR / SSTO), 4))
252377.5808 307203.04 0.8215 0.9064

Interpretation. R2=0.82R^2 = 0.82R2=0.82: lot size accounts for about 82% of the variation in work hours across these runs. The remaining 18% is the residual scatter that other factors (crew, tooling, luck) produce. The correlation is r=0.91r = 0.91r=0.91.

What R-squared does not tell you

Three wrong readings to avoid. First, R2=0.82R^2 = 0.82R2=0.82 does not mean the model is correct or the line is straight; a curved relationship can still post a high R2R^2R2 (this is one lesson of the Anscombe quartet in Chapter 4). Second, it does not mean predictions are accurate to 82%; prediction accuracy is set by sss and the prediction interval, not by R2R^2R2. Third, a high R2R^2R2 does not establish that XXX causes YYY. R2R^2R2 measures how much of YYY’s spread this line removed, nothing more. Always read it beside a residual plot and the standard error sss.

Try it 3.6

A classmate reports R2=0.82R^2 = 0.82R2=0.82 and concludes “the model predicts work hours to within 82%.” Correct this statement, and name the number that actually describes prediction accuracy.

Solution

R2=0.82R^2 = 0.82R2=0.82 means lot size explains 82% of the variation in hours, not that predictions are within 82% of the truth. Prediction accuracy is described by s=MSE=48.8s = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}} = 48.8s=MSE​=48.8 hours, the typical size of a residual, and more completely by a prediction interval, which for a 100-unit run spans about ±105\pm 105±105 hours (3.5 Prediction interval for a new observation). A model can have a high R2R^2R2 and still predict a single case loosely.

3.9 A confidence band for the whole line¶

Intuition¶

The confidence interval of 3.4 Confidence interval for a mean response is honest about one lot size you name in advance. But often you want to draw a band around the entire fitted line and claim, with 95% confidence, that the true line lies inside it everywhere at once. If you simply string together the pointwise intervals, the simultaneous confidence is less than 95%, because you are making many claims and some will fail by chance. The Working-Hotelling band (Definition 3.13) fixes this by using a slightly larger multiplier, so the whole-line claim holds at the stated level.

Formula¶

Definition 3.13: Working-Hotelling confidence band

The Working-Hotelling 100(1−α)%100(1-\alpha)\%100(1−α)% confidence band for the regression line is, at every XhX_hXh​,

Y^h±W s{Y^h},W2=2 F1−α; 2, n−2.\hat{Y}_h \pm W\, s\{\hat{Y}_h\}, \qquad W^2 = 2\, F_{1-\alpha;\,2,\,n-2}.Y^h​±Ws{Y^h​},W2=2F1−α;2,n−2​.

Because W>t1−α/2, n−2W > t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}W>t1−α/2,n−2​, the band covers the entire true line simultaneously at the stated level, at the cost of being a little wider than a pointwise interval everywhere.

In words: the band has the same center and the same standard error s{Y^h}s\{\hat{Y}_h\}s{Y^h​} as the pointwise interval, but the multiplier WWW replaces t1−α/2, n−2t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}t1−α/2,n−2​. Because W>tW > tW>t, the band is a little wider everywhere, and that extra width buys simultaneous coverage of the true line at all XhX_hXh​ at once. The multiplier uses FFF with 2 numerator degrees of freedom because the line has two parameters, β0\beta_0β0​ and β1\beta_1β1​, that can both vary.

W <- sqrt(2 * qf(0.95, 2, n - 2))
round(c(W = W, t = qt(0.975, n - 2)), 4)
     W      t 
2.6162 2.0687 
W = np.sqrt(2 * stats.f.ppf(0.95, 2, n - 2))
print(round(W, 4), round(stats.t.ppf(0.975, n - 2), 4))
2.6162 2.0687

For Toluca the whole-line multiplier is W=2.62W = 2.62W=2.62 against the pointwise t=2.07t = 2.07t=2.07, so the simultaneous band is about 26% wider than a single-point interval. Figure 10 draws both.

Try it 3.7

You plan to read the fitted Toluca line at five different lot sizes and report all five mean-response estimates together. Should you use the pointwise ttt multiplier or the Working-Hotelling WWW? Why?

Solution

Use WWW. Reading the line at five places and claiming all five intervals hold is a simultaneous statement, and stringing pointwise 95% intervals together gives less than 95% joint confidence. The Working-Hotelling band guarantees 95% coverage of the true line at all lot sizes at once, including your five, at the cost of being a little wider. (Bonferroni, an alternative you will meet later, is another way to control the joint error rate.)

3.10 A prediction-focused example: advertising and sales¶

To see the machinery on a fresh dataset, turn to the advertising data: 200 markets’ TV advertising budgets and the resulting product sales (thousands of units). A manager wants to predict sales for a new market that will receive a TV budget of 150 thousand dollars, and to understand the average effect of the budget.

Example 3.7: Predicting sales from a TV budget

Question. Fit sales on TV budget. Is the slope significant? Give the 95% confidence interval for mean sales and the 95% prediction interval for a single new market, both at a TV budget of 150.

Intuition. Same tools as Toluca: a ttt test for the slope, a narrow confidence interval for the mean, a wide prediction interval for one new market.

Formula. Slope test t∗=b1/s{b1}t^{\ast} = b_1/s\{b_1\}t∗=b1​/s{b1​}; intervals Y^h±t s{Y^h}\hat{Y}_h \pm t\, s\{\hat{Y}_h\}Y^h​±ts{Y^h​} and Y^h±t s{pred}\hat{Y}_h \pm t\, s\{\text{pred}\}Y^h​±ts{pred}, now with n−2=198n - 2 = 198n−2=198 degrees of freedom.

Computation.

adv <- read.csv("data/advertising.csv")
fit_adv <- lm(sales ~ TV, data = adv)
summary(fit_adv)$coefficients
predict(fit_adv, data.frame(TV = 150), interval = "confidence")
predict(fit_adv, data.frame(TV = 150), interval = "prediction")
              Estimate  Std. Error  t value    Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 7.03259355 0.457842940 15.36028 1.40630e-35
TV          0.04753664 0.002690607 17.66763 1.46739e-42
       fit      lwr      upr
1 14.16309 13.70842 14.61776
       fit      lwr      upr
1 14.16309 7.720898 20.60528
adv = pd.read_csv("data/advertising.csv")
fit_adv = smf.ols("sales ~ TV", data=adv).fit()
print(fit_adv.summary().tables[1])
pr = fit_adv.get_prediction(pd.DataFrame({"TV": [150]}))
print("mean CI ", pr.conf_int(alpha=0.05))
print("pred PI ", pr.conf_int(obs=True, alpha=0.05))
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept      7.0326      0.458     15.360      0.000       6.130       7.935
TV             0.0475      0.003     17.668      0.000       0.042       0.053
==============================================================================
mean CI  [[13.70842291 14.61775631]]
pred PI  [[ 7.7208979  20.60528133]]

Interpretation. Each extra thousand dollars of TV budget is associated with about 0.0475 thousand more units sold (roughly 48 units), with t∗=17.7t^{\ast} = 17.7t∗=17.7 and a vanishingly small p-value: the effect is clearly real. At a budget of 150, mean sales are estimated at 14.16 thousand units, 95% confidence interval (13.71,14.62)(13.71, 14.62)(13.71,14.62). But a single new market at that budget has a 95% prediction interval of (7.72,20.61)(7.72, 20.61)(7.72,20.61), more than ten times wider. The confidence interval says the average market with this budget sells about 14 thousand units; the prediction interval warns that any one market could sell anywhere from 8 to 21 thousand. For a manager deciding whether to enter one new market, the prediction interval is the honest guide.

Figure 11 draws both bands. Notice the prediction band comfortably contains almost all 200 markets, while the confidence band, being about the mean, does not.

3.11 Chapter summary¶

You can now do inference for a simple linear regression, not just fit it. You saw why the standardized slope and intercept follow a ttt distribution with n−2n-2n−2 degrees of freedom, and used it to build confidence intervals and hypothesis tests for both coefficients (the Toluca slope, t∗=10.29t^{\ast} = 10.29t∗=10.29, 95% CI (2.85,4.29)(2.85, 4.29)(2.85,4.29)). You learned to tell a confidence interval for a mean response from a prediction interval for a single new observation, to derive both variance formulas, and, above all, to say which one a question needs: for a 100-unit run the mean interval is (390,449)(390, 449)(390,449) but the single-run prediction interval is (314,525)(314, 525)(314,525). You derived the ANOVA decomposition, built the table, ran the FFF test (F∗=105.9F^{\ast} = 105.9F∗=105.9), and proved it is the slope ttt test squared. You can read R2=0.82R^2 = 0.82R2=0.82 correctly and widen a pointwise interval into a Working-Hotelling band (W=2.62W = 2.62W=2.62) when you need the whole line.

Key results at a glance.

ResultStatement or formulaValid when
Sampling distributions of b0,b1b_0, b_1b0​,b1​ (Theorem 3.2)(b1−β1)/s{b1}∼tn−2(b_1 - \beta_1)/s\{b_1\} \sim t_{n-2}(b1​−β1​)/s{b1​}∼tn−2​, (b0−β0)/s{b0}∼tn−2(b_0 - \beta_0)/s\{b_0\} \sim t_{n-2}(b0​−β0​)/s{b0​}∼tn−2​SLR model with normal errors
Confidence interval for the slope (Def. 3.3)b1±t1−α/2, n−2 s{b1}b_1 \pm t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}\, s\{b_1\}b1​±t1−α/2,n−2​s{b1​}same; test H0:β1=0H_0:\beta_1=0H0​:β1​=0 via t∗=b1/s{b1}t^{\ast}=b_1/s\{b_1\}t∗=b1​/s{b1​}
Variance of the fitted mean (Theorem 3.5)s2{Y^h}=MSE(1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)s^2\{\hat{Y}_h\} = \mathrm{MSE}(1/n + (X_h-\bar{X})^2/S_{xx})s2{Y^h​}=MSE(1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​)mean response E{Yh}E\{Y_h\}E{Yh​} at fixed XhX_hXh​
Confidence interval for a mean response (Def. 3.4)Y^h±t1−α/2, n−2 s{Y^h}\hat{Y}_h \pm t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}\, s\{\hat{Y}_h\}Y^h​±t1−α/2,n−2​s{Y^h​}averaging over many cases at XhX_hXh​
Variance of a prediction (Theorem 3.7)s2{pred}=MSE(1+1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)s^2\{\text{pred}\} = \mathrm{MSE}(1 + 1/n + (X_h-\bar{X})^2/S_{xx})s2{pred}=MSE(1+1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​)new case’s error independent of the fit
Prediction interval (Def. 3.6)Y^h±t1−α/2, n−2 s{pred}\hat{Y}_h \pm t_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-2}\, s\{\text{pred}\}Y^h​±t1−α/2,n−2​s{pred}one single new observation at XhX_hXh​
ANOVA decomposition (Theorem 3.9)SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE, df n−1=1+(n−2)n-1 = 1 + (n-2)n−1=1+(n−2)least-squares fit with an intercept
Expected mean squares (Theorem 3.10)E{MSE}=σ2E\{\mathrm{MSE}\}=\sigma^2E{MSE}=σ2, E{MSR}=σ2+β12SxxE\{\mathrm{MSR}\}=\sigma^2+\beta_1^2 S_{xx}E{MSR}=σ2+β12​Sxx​SLR model
FFF test and F=t2F = t^2F=t2 (Theorem 3.11)F∗=MSR/MSE∼F1, n−2F^{\ast}=\mathrm{MSR}/\mathrm{MSE}\sim F_{1,\,n-2}F∗=MSR/MSE∼F1,n−2​, F∗=(t∗)2F^{\ast}=(t^{\ast})^2F∗=(t∗)2normal errors; testing H0:β1=0H_0:\beta_1=0H0​:β1​=0
Coefficient of determination (Def. 3.12)R2=SSR/SSTO=1−SSE/SSTO=r2R^2 = \mathrm{SSR}/\mathrm{SSTO} = 1 - \mathrm{SSE}/\mathrm{SSTO} = r^2R2=SSR/SSTO=1−SSE/SSTO=r2describes variation explained, not fit correctness
Working-Hotelling band (Def. 3.13)Y^h±W s{Y^h}\hat{Y}_h \pm W\, s\{\hat{Y}_h\}Y^h​±Ws{Y^h​}, W2=2F1−α; 2, n−2W^2 = 2 F_{1-\alpha;\,2,\,n-2}W2=2F1−α;2,n−2​simultaneous coverage of the whole line

Key terms. Estimated standard error, confidence interval for the slope, hypothesis test for the slope, confidence interval for a mean response, prediction interval, analysis of variance, total sum of squares, regression sum of squares, mean square, FFF test, coefficient of determination, Working-Hotelling confidence band, degrees of freedom.

You should now be able to:

  • Derive the sampling distributions of b0b_0b0​ and b1b_1b1​ and explain why inference uses tn−2t_{n-2}tn−2​.

  • Construct and interpret confidence intervals and hypothesis tests for the slope and intercept from software output.

  • Distinguish a confidence interval for a mean response from a prediction interval for a new observation, derive both, and choose the right one.

  • Derive the ANOVA decomposition SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE, build the ANOVA table, and run the FFF test for the slope.

  • Prove that F∗=(t∗)2F^{\ast} = (t^{\ast})^2F∗=(t∗)2 in simple linear regression.

  • Interpret R2R^2R2 correctly, and name what it does and does not measure.

  • Construct a Working-Hotelling confidence band for the whole regression line.

Where this fits. In the workflow spine of The modeling workflow (ASK, EXPLORE, FIT, CHECK, USE), this chapter is the heart of the USE stage: it turns a fitted line into decisions and honest forecasts with stated uncertainty. Chapter 2 gave the point estimates; here we attached intervals and tests. The prediction interval (3.5 Prediction interval for a new observation) returns in Chapter 12 when we validate models on held-out data and in Chapter 15 when we forecast, where we will confront why time-ordered data makes prediction intervals harder. The ANOVA table (3.6 The analysis of variance approach) and the FFF test (3.7 The F test and its equivalence to the t test) generalize in Chapter 8 into the extra-sums-of-squares machinery (8.3 Extra sums of squares) and the general linear test (8.4 The general linear test), which can compare whole models at once. And the tiny slope p-value here is the benchmark Chapter 5 checks against a permutation test that assumes no normality at all (5.2 The permutation test for the slope), asking whether our ttt-based conclusion survives without the normal error model.

3.12 Frequently asked questions¶

Q1. What is the one-sentence difference between a confidence interval and a prediction interval? A confidence interval is for the mean response at XhX_hXh​, averaged over all cases at that predictor value; a prediction interval is for a single new case, and is wider because it must also cover that case’s own random scatter σ2\sigma^2σ2.

Q2. Why does the interval widen as XhX_hXh​ moves away from Xˉ\bar{X}Xˉ? Because the variance of the fitted mean carries a (Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx(X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx}(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​ term. Away from the center, a small change in the estimated slope pivots the line a large vertical distance, so the fitted value there is less certain. Every fitted line passes through (Xˉ,Yˉ)(\bar{X}, \bar{Y})(Xˉ,Yˉ), so the uncertainty is smallest there.

Q3. Why use ttt and not the normal distribution? Because we estimate σ\sigmaσ with s=MSEs = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}s=MSE​ rather than knowing it. That extra estimation makes the reference distribution tn−2t_{n-2}tn−2​, with heavier tails than the normal. For large nnn the two nearly coincide, but for n=25n = 25n=25 the difference matters.

Q4. The FFF test and the ttt test gave the same p-value. Do I need both? In simple linear regression, no: F∗=(t∗)2F^{\ast} = (t^{\ast})^2F∗=(t∗)2, so they are one test. Report the ttt test for a single slope because it keeps the direction (sign) of the effect. The FFF test earns its keep in multiple regression, where it can test several coefficients simultaneously.

Q5. My R2R^2R2 is high. Does that mean my model is good? Not by itself. A high R2R^2R2 can accompany a curved relationship, non-constant variance, or influential outliers, all of which a residual plot would reveal. R2R^2R2 measures variation explained, not correctness, not prediction accuracy, and not causation. Read it alongside sss and diagnostic plots (Chapter 9).

Q6. Can I use these intervals to predict hours for a lot of 500 units? No. That is extrapolation far beyond the data range (20 to 120 units). The formulas will happily produce an interval, but its stated confidence assumes the straight-line model holds out there, which the data cannot support. Trust intervals only inside, or just barely outside, the observed range of XXX.

Q7. What exactly does “95% confident” mean for the slope interval? It means the procedure traps the true slope in 95% of repeated samples, as Figure 12 shows with 100 simulated intervals. For your one interval (2.85,4.29)(2.85, 4.29)(2.85,4.29), the true slope is either in it or not; the 95% describes the long-run reliability of the method, not a probability about this particular interval.

3.13 Practice problems¶

Note

Unless a problem says otherwise, use toluca.csv and the fitted model Y^=62.37+3.5702 X\hat{Y} = 62.37 + 3.5702\,XY^=62.37+3.5702X with n=25n = 25n=25, Xˉ=70\bar{X} = 70Xˉ=70, Sxx=19,800S_{xx} = 19{,}800Sxx​=19,800, MSE=2384\mathrm{MSE} = 2384MSE=2384, s{b1}=0.347s\{b_1\} = 0.347s{b1​}=0.347, s{b0}=26.18s\{b_0\} = 26.18s{b0​}=26.18, and t0.975, 23=2.069t_{0.975,\,23} = 2.069t0.975,23​=2.069. Problems are marked (A) concepts, (B) theory, or (C) data analysis. Odd-numbered answers appear in Appendix H; full solutions are in the instructor materials.

  1. (A) In one sentence each, state what a confidence interval for the mean response and a prediction interval for a new observation are about, and say which is wider and why.

  2. (A) The slope’s 95% confidence interval is (2.85,4.29)(2.85, 4.29)(2.85,4.29). Explain what “95% confident” means here, and what it does not mean.

  3. (A) Explain why the confidence interval for a mean response is narrowest at Xh=XˉX_h = \bar{X}Xh​=Xˉ and widens on both sides.

  4. (A) Why does inference for the slope use a ttt distribution with n−2n-2n−2 degrees of freedom rather than a standard normal? Where do the n−2n-2n−2 degrees of freedom come from?

  5. (A) A report states “R2=0.82R^2 = 0.82R2=0.82, so the model is correct.” Give two distinct reasons this conclusion does not follow.

  6. (A) The intercept’s 95% interval (8.21,116.52)(8.21, 116.52)(8.21,116.52) is enormously wide compared with the slope’s. Explain why, referring to where X=0X = 0X=0 sits relative to the data.

  7. (A) In simple linear regression the ANOVA FFF test and the slope ttt test always agree. State the exact relationship between the two statistics and one reason to still report the ttt test.

  8. (A) A colleague uses a 95% confidence interval for the mean response to budget a single upcoming production run. Explain why this understates the risk, and which interval they should use.

  9. (B) Derive the ttt statistic (b1−β1)/s{b1}∼tn−2(b_1 - \beta_1)/s\{b_1\} \sim t_{n-2}(b1​−β1​)/s{b1​}∼tn−2​ (Theorem 3.2), stating clearly which facts you take from Chapter 2, which you take on faith until Chapter 7, and why the combination is a ttt.

  10. (B) Derive Var⁡{Y^h}=σ2(1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)\operatorname{Var}\{\hat{Y}_h\} = \sigma^2(1/n + (X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx})Var{Y^h​}=σ2(1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​) (Theorem 3.5), including the step that Yˉ\bar{Y}Yˉ and b1b_1b1​ are uncorrelated.

  11. (B) Derive the prediction-error variance σ2(1+1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)\sigma^2(1 + 1/n + (X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx})σ2(1+1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​) (Theorem 3.7), explaining where the leading 1 comes from and why the new error is independent of Y^h\hat{Y}_hY^h​.

  12. (B) Prove the ANOVA identity SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE (Theorem 3.9) by showing the cross term vanishes, naming the residual identities you use.

  13. (B) Prove that F∗=(t∗)2F^{\ast} = (t^{\ast})^2F∗=(t∗)2 in simple linear regression (Theorem 3.11), starting from SSR=b12Sxx\mathrm{SSR} = b_1^2 S_{xx}SSR=b12​Sxx​.

  14. (B) Show that E{MSR}=σ2+β12SxxE\{\mathrm{MSR}\} = \sigma^2 + \beta_1^2 S_{xx}E{MSR}=σ2+β12​Sxx​ (Theorem 3.10), and explain why this makes MSR/MSE\mathrm{MSR}/\mathrm{MSE}MSR/MSE a sensible test statistic for H0:β1=0H_0: \beta_1 = 0H0​:β1​=0.

  15. (B) Show that a 100(1−α)%100(1-\alpha)\%100(1−α)% confidence interval for β1\beta_1β1​ excludes 0 exactly when the two-sided ttt test of H0:β1=0H_0: \beta_1 = 0H0​:β1​=0 rejects at level α\alphaα.

  16. (B) Starting from R2=1−SSE/SSTOR^2 = 1 - \mathrm{SSE}/\mathrm{SSTO}R2=1−SSE/SSTO and SSE=Syy−b12Sxx\mathrm{SSE} = S_{yy} - b_1^2 S_{xx}SSE=Syy​−b12​Sxx​, show that R2=b12Sxx/SyyR^2 = b_1^2 S_{xx}/S_{yy}R2=b12​Sxx​/Syy​, and hence that R2=r2R^2 = r^2R2=r2 where r=Sxy/SxxSyyr = S_{xy}/\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{yy}}r=Sxy​/Sxx​Syy​​.

  17. (B) The Working-Hotelling multiplier is W=2F1−α;2,n−2W = \sqrt{2 F_{1-\alpha;2,n-2}}W=2F1−α;2,n−2​​ and the pointwise multiplier is t1−α/2,n−2t_{1-\alpha/2,n-2}t1−α/2,n−2​. Explain, without a full proof, why WWW must exceed ttt and what the extra width buys.

  18. (B) Show that the prediction interval half-width never shrinks below t st\, sts even as n→∞n \to \inftyn→∞, while the confidence-interval half-width shrinks to 0. What does this say about the limits of prediction?

  19. (C) Fit the Toluca model in R or Python. Reproduce the slope’s ttt statistic, its p-value, and its 95% confidence interval, and confirm they match the values in this chapter.

  20. (C) Compute a 95% confidence interval for the mean hours at Xh=40X_h = 40Xh​=40 units and at Xh=90X_h = 90Xh​=90 units. Which is wider, and does the difference match the (Xh−Xˉ)2(X_h - \bar{X})^2(Xh​−Xˉ)2 rule?

  21. (C) Compute both the 95% confidence interval for the mean and the 95% prediction interval for a new run at Xh=50X_h = 50Xh​=50 units. Report the ratio of their widths and explain it.

  22. (C) Build the ANOVA table for Toluca in software. From it, extract SSR\mathrm{SSR}SSR, SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE, MSR\mathrm{MSR}MSR, MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE, and F∗F^{\ast}F∗, and verify F∗=(t∗)2F^{\ast} = (t^{\ast})^2F∗=(t∗)2 using the slope ttt from problem 19.

  23. (C) Using the advertising data, fit sales ~ TV, test the slope, and give a 95% prediction interval for a new market with a TV budget of 200. Interpret the interval for a manager.

  24. (C) Write a seeded simulation (seed 4210, 2000 datasets from the fitted Toluca model at the observed lot sizes) that checks the empirical coverage of the 95% confidence interval for the mean at Xh=60X_h = 60Xh​=60. Report the coverage and compare it with 0.95.

  25. (C) Reproduce the Working-Hotelling multiplier WWW and the pointwise ttt multiplier for Toluca. Then compute both the pointwise 95% interval and the Working-Hotelling band half-width at Xh=120X_h = 120Xh​=120, and report the ratio.

  26. (C) Regress hours ~ lotsize and make a plot of the fitted line with the 95% confidence band and the 95% prediction band (as in Figure 1). Describe how the two bands differ in width and shape.

  27. (B) A student claims that because F∗=105.9F^{\ast} = 105.9F∗=105.9 is “huge,” the model must predict individual runs accurately. Explain the confusion between the FFF test (about the slope) and prediction accuracy (about sss and the prediction interval), using the Toluca numbers.

  28. (C) Using the advertising data, compute R2R^2R2 two ways: from the ANOVA sums of squares and as the square of the correlation cor(adv$TV, adv$sales). Confirm they match, and interpret the value.

3.14 Exam practice¶

The problems above build skills one at a time. Exams ask you to combine them and, above all, to explain in full sentences: to read software output in context, to judge a claim, and to say what would change under a new condition. The five problems below are written in that exam voice. Work each one as if for full marks, in complete sentences, before opening the model answer. A bare number or a one-word verdict earns little credit on the real thing; the reasoning is the answer.

EP 3.1 (interpret output in context). A production scheduler has one specific new run of 80 units on next week’s calendar. Software fits the Toluca model and returns two intervals at a lot size of 80.

predict(fit, data.frame(lotsize = 80), interval = "confidence")
predict(fit, data.frame(lotsize = 80), interval = "prediction")
      fit      lwr      upr
1 347.982 326.5449 369.4191
      fit      lwr      upr
1 347.982 244.7333 451.2307
pr = fit.get_prediction(pd.DataFrame({"lotsize": [80]}))
print("mean CI ", pr.conf_int(alpha=0.05))
print("pred PI ", pr.conf_int(obs=True, alpha=0.05))
mean CI  [[326.54493825 369.41910215]]
pred PI  [[244.73334785 451.23069256]]

Interpret each interval in one sentence, with units and in the context of this problem. Then the scheduler proposes to budget next week’s single run at “between 327 and 369 hours, 95% sure,” using the first interval. Explain in full sentences whether that is the right interval to use, which one the scheduler should use instead, and why the two intervals differ by so much.

Model answer

The first interval, (326.5,369.4)(326.5, 369.4)(326.5,369.4) hours, is a 95% confidence interval for the mean hours across all runs of 80 units: we are 95% confident that the average time for 80-unit runs, taken over many such runs, lies between about 327 and 369 hours. The second interval, (244.7,451.2)(244.7, 451.2)(244.7,451.2) hours, is a 95% prediction interval for a single new 80-unit run: we are 95% confident that one specific new run of that size will take between about 245 and 451 hours. The scheduler’s plan is wrong, and it will understate the risk. The confidence interval describes where the long-run average lands, but next week’s job is one run, not an average, and a single run carries its own random scatter of about s=48.8s = 48.8s=48.8 hours on top of our uncertainty about the line. The scheduler should budget with the prediction interval, (245,451)(245, 451)(245,451) hours, which is roughly 206 hours wide against the confidence interval’s 43 hours. The two differ because the prediction interval adds the new run’s own error variance σ2\sigma^2σ2 (the leading 1 in s2{pred}=MSE(1+1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)s^2\{\text{pred}\} = \mathrm{MSE}(1 + 1/n + (X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx})s2{pred}=MSE(1+1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​)), a term that never goes away no matter how much data we collect.

A weak answer labels the two intervals correctly but does not explain that a single run needs the prediction interval because it carries the extra σ2\sigma^2σ2 scatter, and so misses why the confidence interval would blow the budget.

EP 3.2 (a student claims X, evaluate). A classmate writes: “Under the normal-error model the slope estimate b1b_1b1​ is exactly normal, so a 95% confidence interval for the slope should use the standard normal multiplier 1.96.” The chapter instead uses t0.975, 23=2.069t_{0.975,\,23} = 2.069t0.975,23​=2.069. Explain in full sentences what is right and what is wrong in the classmate’s reasoning, what specific step forces the switch from 1.96 to 2.069, and what happens to the gap between the two multipliers as the sample size grows.

Model answer

The classmate is right that b1b_1b1​ is exactly normal under the normal-error model: it is a linear combination of independent normal responses, so b1∼N(β1,σ2/Sxx)b_1 \sim N(\beta_1, \sigma^2/S_{xx})b1​∼N(β1​,σ2/Sxx​). If we knew the error standard deviation σ\sigmaσ, then (b1−β1)/(σ/Sxx)(b_1 - \beta_1)/(\sigma/\sqrt{S_{xx}})(b1​−β1​)/(σ/Sxx​​) would be exactly standard normal and 1.96 would be the correct multiplier. The flaw is that we do not know σ\sigmaσ. We estimate it by s=MSEs = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}s=MSE​ and divide by the estimated standard error s{b1}=MSE/Sxxs\{b_1\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}/S_{xx}}s{b1​}=MSE/Sxx​​ instead. Replacing the fixed constant σ\sigmaσ with the noisy estimate sss injects extra uncertainty, and that is exactly what turns the reference distribution from a normal into a ttt with n−2=23n - 2 = 23n−2=23 degrees of freedom, whose heavier tails require the larger multiplier 2.069 to still capture 95% of the distribution. As nnn grows, MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE estimates σ2\sigma^2σ2 more and more reliably, the ttt distribution approaches the normal, and the multiplier shrinks back toward 1.96, so the gap closes.

A weak answer just states “we use ttt because nnn is small” without naming the substitution of sss for σ\sigmaσ as the reason, and so cannot explain why the gap vanishes as nnn grows.

EP 3.3 (interpret output in context, evaluate a claim). The advertising data record TV budgets and sales for n=200n = 200n=200 markets. Fitting sales ~ TV gives the output below.

summary(fit_adv)$coefficients
anova(fit_adv)
c(R2 = summary(fit_adv)$r.squared, s = summary(fit_adv)$sigma)
             Estimate Std. Error  t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)  7.032594 0.45784294 15.36028        0
TV           0.047537 0.00269061 17.66763        0
           Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)
TV          1 3314.6  3314.6  312.14 < 2.2e-16
Residuals 198 2102.5    10.6
        R2          s 
    0.6119     3.2587 
print(sm.stats.anova_lm(fit_adv))
print(round(fit_adv.rsquared, 4), round(np.sqrt(fit_adv.scale), 4))
             df       sum_sq      mean_sq           F        PR(>F)
TV          1.0  3314.618167  3314.618167  312.144994  1.467390e-42
Residual  198.0  2102.530583    10.618841           NaN           NaN
0.6119 3.2587

First, verify from the numbers that the FFF statistic equals the square of the TV slope’s ttt statistic, and explain in one sentence why that identity must hold here. Then a manager reads F=312F = 312F=312 and R2=0.61R^2 = 0.61R2=0.61 and concludes, “the model is so significant that it will predict any one market’s sales to within a unit or two.” Explain in full sentences what the large FFF actually establishes, what R2=0.61R^2 = 0.61R2=0.61 means, and which number in the output governs how far a single new market can fall from the line.

Model answer

The slope’s ttt statistic is 17.66763, and its square is 17.667632=312.1417.66763^2 = 312.1417.667632=312.14, which matches the FFF value in the ANOVA table to the digits shown. This identity must hold because in simple linear regression F∗=(t∗)2F^{\ast} = (t^{\ast})^2F∗=(t∗)2: the FFF test and the two-sided slope ttt test are the same test, so the FFF statistic carries no information the ttt statistic did not. The large FFF, like the tiny slope p-value, establishes only that the slope is far from zero relative to its standard error, that is, that TV budget is genuinely related to sales; it says nothing about how tightly the points hug the line.
