The value R2=0.61R^2 = 0.61R2=0.61 means that TV budget accounts for about 61% of the variation in sales across markets, leaving 39% to other factors, but a proportion of variation explained is not a promise of accurate individual prediction. The number that governs how far one new market can land from the line is the residual standard error s=3.2587s = 3.2587s=3.2587 thousand units, the typical size of a single market’s scatter about the line; it feeds the prediction interval, which the chapter found spans roughly (7.7,20.6)(7.7, 20.6)(7.7,20.6) thousand units at a budget of 150 (Example 3.7). So the manager’s “within a unit or two” is off by an order of magnitude: a significant, high-FFF model can still predict a single market only loosely.

A weak answer confirms F=t2F = t^2F=t2 but then treats the large FFF or the high R2R^2R2 as evidence of prediction accuracy, missing that sss (and the prediction interval built from it), not FFF or R2R^2R2, sets how far one case can miss.

EP 3.4 (what would change if). Suppose the Toluca engineers gathered far more production data at the same spread of lot sizes, so that the sample size nnn grows very large while SxxS_{xx}Sxx​ grows in proportion and MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE stays near 2384. Explain in full sentences what happens, as n→∞n \to \inftyn→∞, to (a) the width of the 95% confidence interval for the mean hours at Xh=100X_h = 100Xh​=100, and (b) the width of the 95% prediction interval for a single new 100-unit run. Say why the two behave differently, and what that difference reveals about the limits of prediction.

Model answer

The confidence interval for the mean uses s2{Y^h}=MSE(1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)s^2\{\hat{Y}_h\} = \mathrm{MSE}(1/n + (X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx})s2{Y^h​}=MSE(1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​). As n→∞n \to \inftyn→∞ with SxxS_{xx}Sxx​ growing in proportion, both 1/n1/n1/n and (Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx(X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx}(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​ shrink to zero, so s{Y^h}s\{\hat{Y}_h\}s{Y^h​} and the interval’s width shrink to zero: with unlimited data we can pin down the average hours at 100 units as precisely as we like, because the line itself becomes perfectly determined. The prediction interval uses s2{pred}=MSE(1+1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)s^2\{\text{pred}\} = \mathrm{MSE}(1 + 1/n + (X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx})s2{pred}=MSE(1+1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​). The same two terms vanish, but the leading 1 does not, so the prediction variance falls only to MSE≈2384\mathrm{MSE} \approx 2384MSE≈2384, and the interval’s half-width settles near t⋅s≈1.96×48.8≈96t \cdot s \approx 1.96 \times 48.8 \approx 96t⋅s≈1.96×48.8≈96 hours rather than to zero. The two behave differently because the prediction interval must cover a single new run’s own random scatter σ2\sigma^2σ2, which no amount of data removes, whereas the confidence interval only chases the position of the line. The lesson is that prediction has a hard floor: even a perfectly estimated model cannot forecast one new run better than that run’s own irreducible variability allows.

A weak answer says “both intervals get narrower” without recognizing that the confidence interval goes to zero while the prediction interval stops at the irreducible σ2\sigma^2σ2 floor.

EP 3.5 (interpret a figure, explain why). Figure 4 plots two estimated-standard-error curves against lot size: the mean-response standard error s{Y^h}s\{\hat{Y}_h\}s{Y^h​} (blue) dips to a minimum near the mean lot size of 70 and rises toward both ends, while the prediction standard error s{pred}s\{\text{pred}\}s{pred} (yellow) stays nearly flat near 49 hours across the whole range and sits above the blue curve everywhere. Explain in full sentences (a) why the blue curve is U-shaped with its lowest point at Xˉ\bar{X}Xˉ, and (b) why the yellow curve barely bends even though it lies above the blue one at every lot size.

Model answer

The blue curve tracks s{Y^h}=MSE(1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)s\{\hat{Y}_h\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}(1/n + (X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx})}s{Y^h​}=MSE(1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​)​. The only part that depends on where you predict is (Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx(X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx}(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​, which is zero when Xh=Xˉ=70X_h = \bar{X} = 70Xh​=Xˉ=70 and grows as XhX_hXh​ moves away in either direction. That squared distance makes the curve U-shaped, with its minimum exactly at the mean lot size, because the fitted line is pinned down most tightly at the center of the data and a small tilt in the slope swings it more the further out you go. The yellow curve tracks s{pred}=MSE(1+1/n+(Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx)s\{\text{pred}\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}(1 + 1/n + (X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx})}s{pred}=MSE(1+1/n+(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​)​, which has the same position-dependent term but adds the leading 1. That leading term contributes MSE≈2384\mathrm{MSE} \approx 2384MSE≈2384 to the variance, far larger than the 1/n1/n1/n and (Xh−Xˉ)2/Sxx(X_h - \bar{X})^2/S_{xx}(Xh​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​ pieces across the observed range, so the total is dominated by a constant and s{pred}s\{\text{pred}\}s{pred} stays close to 2384≈48.8\sqrt{2384} \approx 48.82384​≈48.8 hours everywhere. The curve still bends upward slightly at the extremes for the same reason the blue one does, but the bend is tiny relative to the flat floor set by a single run’s own scatter.

A weak answer says the prediction curve is “just higher because prediction is wider” without identifying the leading 1 (the run’s own variance σ2\sigma^2σ2) as the large, position-independent term that flattens the curve.

3.15 Chapter game¶

Play the Chapter 3 game

Play the Chapter 3 game on your phone or laptop: 10 quick rounds, no setup. Open the game.

It drills the chapter’s decisions: reading a slope test and confidence interval, telling a mean-response interval from a prediction interval, ordering the ANOVA table, fitting a line to real Toluca runs, and using F=t2F = t^2F=t2, with the reason shown after every answer.

Resumen del capítulo (en español)

Este capítulo hace inferencia (inference) para la regresión lineal simple, continuando con los datos de la Toluca Company del Capítulo 2 (recta ajustada Y^=62.37+3.5702 X\hat{Y} = 62.37 + 3.5702\,XY^=62.37+3.5702X, MSE=2384\mathrm{MSE} = 2384MSE=2384). El Capítulo 2 dio estimaciones puntuales; aquí añadimos incertidumbre en forma de intervalos y pruebas.

Bajo el modelo de errores normales, los estimadores b0b_0b0​ y b1b_1b1​ son exactamente normales, y al estandarizar con el error estándar estimado s{b1}=MSE/Sxxs\{b_1\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}/S_{xx}}s{b1​}=MSE/Sxx​​ se obtiene una distribución t (t distribution) con n−2n-2n−2 grados de libertad. De ahí salen el intervalo de confianza (confidence interval) para la pendiente, b1±t s{b1}=(2.85,4.29)b_1 \pm t\,s\{b_1\} = (2.85, 4.29)b1​±ts{b1​}=(2.85,4.29), y la prueba de hipótesis (hypothesis test) de H0:β1=0H_0: \beta_1 = 0H0​:β1​=0, con t∗=10.29t^{\ast} = 10.29t∗=10.29 y valor p de 4.45×10−104.45\times10^{-10}4.45×10−10: el tamaño del lote sí afecta a las horas.

La distinción central del capítulo es entre el intervalo de confianza para la respuesta media (mean response) y el intervalo de predicción (prediction interval) para una observación nueva. El primero describe el promedio de muchas corridas; el segundo suma la varianza propia de la corrida nueva y por eso es mucho más ancho. Para un lote de 100 unidades, el intervalo de la media es (390,449)(390, 449)(390,449) y el de predicción es (314,525)(314, 525)(314,525). Una simulación con semilla confirma que cada uno cubre su objetivo cerca del 95%.

El enfoque de análisis de varianza (analysis of variance) descompone SSTO=SSR+SSE\mathrm{SSTO} = \mathrm{SSR} + \mathrm{SSE}SSTO=SSR+SSE (307,203=252,378+54,825307{,}203 = 252{,}378 + 54{,}825307,203=252,378+54,825) y produce la prueba F (F test), con F∗=105.9F^{\ast} = 105.9F∗=105.9. Demostramos que en regresión simple F∗=(t∗)2F^{\ast} = (t^{\ast})^2F∗=(t∗)2: son la misma prueba. El coeficiente de determinación (coefficient of determination) R2=0.82R^2 = 0.82R2=0.82 es la proporción de variación explicada, no una medida de exactitud ni de causalidad. Por último, la banda de Working-Hotelling (Working-Hotelling band), con multiplicador W=2.62W = 2.62W=2.62, da confianza simultánea para toda la recta a la vez.

Part I: Regression from the ground up
2. Simple linear regression
